title: Dog Thick Green Nasal Discharge: Causes & Triage Guide
meta_desc: Thick green mucus from your dog's nose often indicates a bacterial infection. Learn when to seek veterinary care and how a photo helps with triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Thick green nasal discharge usually indicates an infection or foreign object requiring a vet visit within 24–48 hours. Seek emergency care immediately if it is accompanied by difficulty breathing, high fever, or lethargy.

What does thick green nasal discharge mean for my dog?

Thick, green, or yellow nasal discharge, known as purulent discharge, signifies that the immune system is actively fighting an infection. Common causes include:

  • Bacterial or fungal infections
  • Dental disease
  • A foreign object, such as a grass seed, lodged in the nasal passage

Is thick green nasal discharge a veterinary emergency?

The urgency level for this symptom is Medium. While not usually an immediate life-threatening emergency, you should schedule a vet appointment within 24 to 48 hours, or contact an emergency clinic sooner if you notice:

  • Difficulty breathing
  • A persistent high fever
  • Extreme lethargy

How does taking a photo of my dog's nose help the vet?

A clear photo helps the veterinarian or triage nurse determine the potential cause and urgency. Visual evidence allows the medical team to assess:

  • The exact color and viscosity of the mucus
  • Whether the discharge is unilateral (one nostril) or bilateral (both nostrils)
  • The severity of symptoms to help prioritize your dog's appointment

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, a thick green nasal discharge can be indicative of several underlying conditions. An acute unilateral nasal discharge may suggest a foreign body, while a chronic nasal discharge that progresses from unilateral to bilateral or changes in character (e.g., from mucopurulent to serosanguineous or hemorrhagic) may indicate neoplastic or mycotic disease. Diagnostics should include a thorough history and physical examination, advanced imaging such as CT scans and radiographs, rhinoscopy, nasal biopsy, and deep nasal tissue culture to identify the etiology, such as fungal rhinitis, neoplasia, or foreign body. While bacterial rhinitis is rare as a primary condition in dogs, it can occur secondary to other nasal conditions; therefore, bacterial cultures may be warranted. Cytologic evaluation can aid in diagnosing nasal fungal infections, and serologic testing for fungal infections may be considered, although results should be interpreted cautiously.

Chapter: Respiratory, Infectious Diseases, Neoplasia, Fungal

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1489)

faqs.question: Can I give my dog human decongestants for nasal discharge?
faqs.answer: No. Many human cold and flu medications contain ingredients like pseudoephedrine or acetaminophen, which are highly toxic to dogs. Never administer medication without veterinary approval.

faqs.question: Is green nasal discharge contagious to other pets?
faqs.answer: It can be. If the discharge is caused by a respiratory infection like Kennel Cough or Canine Influenza, it can be highly contagious to other dogs in the household.

faqs.question: What if there is blood mixed with the green mucus?
faqs.answer: If you notice blood, the urgency increases. Blood can indicate severe inflammation, a foreign body, or more serious issues within the nasal cavity that require prompt examination.
